title: How to make decisions from preference instead of fear
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
I spent years making decisions like a cornered animal 

 Not metaphorically. Actually. My nervous system would hijack every choice, big or small, and turn...
htmlContent:
I spent years making decisions like a cornered animal

Not metaphorically. Actually. My nervous system would hijack every choice, big or small, and turn it into a survival scenario. Should I take this job? That wasn't a question about salary or growth. It was a question about safety. Should I end this relationship? That wasn't about compatibility or happiness. It was about whether I could survive the fallout.

Here's the thing. When you grow up in chaos, your brain learns that every decision carries life-or-death weight. You don't get to choose from preference. You choose from fear. From vigilance. From the desperate need to control outcomes because the alternative feels like annihilation.

I remember standing in a grocery store aisle for twenty minutes once. Just trying to pick a brand of pasta sauce. My body was tight, my jaw clenched, my mind racing through all the wrong choices I could make. It sounds ridiculous when I say it out loud. But it wasn't ridiculous to my nervous system. It was doing its job.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body, not in the story we tell about it. And when trauma lives in your body, it shapes every decision you make. You're not choosing from what you want. You're choosing from what won't get you hurt. And those two things are very, very different.

So how do you start making decisions from preference instead of fear? How do you teach a nervous system that's been trained for survival that it can actually rest, that it can choose what it wants instead of what's safe?

I'm not going to tell you it's easy. It's not. But it's possible. And I want to walk you through what I've learned, what's helped me, and what's still hard.

The fear decision machine

Let me describe what a fear-based decision looks like in real time. You have a choice to make. Maybe it's something small, like whether to speak up in a meeting. Maybe it's something bigger, like whether to move to a new city. Your mind starts generating options, but they all feel wrong. You scan for threats. You imagine every worst-case scenario. You feel tight in your chest, shallow in your breath, hot or cold in your skin.

And then you make a choice. But it doesn't feel like a choice. It feels like surrender. Like you're picking the option that hurts the least, that exposes you the least, that keeps you small and safe and invisible.

That's the fear decision machine. And it runs on a very specific fuel: the belief that you cannot handle what happens next.

Bessel van der Kolk, who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades studying how trauma changes the brain's ability to process information. One thing he found is that trauma survivors often have trouble with what's called "interoception" - the ability to sense what's happening inside your own body. And here's why that matters for decision making. If you can't feel what you actually want, if your internal signals are scrambled or silent, then you have nothing to guide you except fear.

Preference comes from the body. It comes from a felt sense of "I want this" or "I don't want this" that isn't tangled up with survival. But when your body is still in survival mode, that felt sense is buried under layers of vigilance.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on emotional flashbacks and the inner critic helped me understand that my fear-based decisions weren't character flaws. They were symptoms. They were my system trying to protect me from pain that had already happened.

So the first step isn't to stop making fear-based decisions. That's like telling someone with a broken leg to stop limping. The first step is to recognize that you're limping. To notice the fear without judging it. To say, "Oh, I'm making this choice from fear right now. That's okay. I just want to know that's what's happening."

Finding your preference signal

Here's where it gets tricky. If you've been making decisions from fear for years, or decades, you might not know what your preferences actually are. You might have forgotten that you even have preferences. You might look at a menu and feel nothing. You might look at a career path and feel nothing. You might look at a relationship and feel nothing except a vague sense of dread or obligation.

That's not because you're broken. It's because your preference signal has been drowned out by fear for so long that it's gone quiet. It's like a radio station that's still broadcasting, but the static is so loud you can't hear the music.

So how do you find it again?

You start small. Really small. You practice making decisions that don't matter, decisions where the worst possible outcome is mildly inconvenient. What do you want for breakfast? Not what's safest, not what's easiest, not what you "should" eat. What do you actually want? Stay with me here. This feels trivial. It's not. Every time you make a small choice from preference, you're rewiring your nervous system. You're telling your body that it's safe enough to want things.

I started with coffee orders. For years I ordered the same thing every time because making a different choice felt like too much risk. What if I didn't like it? What if I wasted money? What if I looked stupid? One day I decided to try something new. I felt the fear in my chest. But I ordered it anyway. And I didn't die. And I didn't hate it. And something shifted.

That's the kind of practice that rebuilds the preference signal. It's not about big life-changing decisions. It's about the small, daily, mundane choices that remind your body that choice can be safe.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Richard Schwartz's Internal Family Systems model helped me understand that the part of me that makes fear-based decisions isn't my enemy. It's a protector. It's trying to keep me safe. And when I started listening to that part instead of fighting it, the fear started to loosen.

The difference between safety and comfort

This is a distinction that changed everything for me. Safety and comfort are not the same thing. Your nervous system might be telling you that you need comfort when what you actually need is safety. And safety doesn't always feel comfortable.

Think about it. Making a decision from preference often feels uncomfortable at first. It feels risky. It feels exposed. Your body might react with anxiety, with fear, with that familiar tightness in your chest. But that doesn't mean you're not safe. It means you're doing something new. And new things feel scary to a traumatized nervous system.

Deb Dana, who studies Polyvagal Theory and its application in clinical work, talks about something called "glimmers." These are small moments of safety and connection that your nervous system can learn to notice. And here's the thing. You can create glimmers intentionally. You can make a small decision from preference and then notice what happens in your body. You might feel a slight relaxation. A sense of ease. A quiet "yes" that wasn't there before.

That's your preference signal coming back online.

But you have to be willing to tolerate the discomfort of the learning curve. You have to be willing to make decisions that don't feel safe to your fear-based parts, even when your whole body is screaming at you to retreat.

A book that many find validates their childhood experience is Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link). Lindsay Gibson's work helped me understand why my preferences were so often invalidated or ignored growing up. When you're raised by people who can't handle your autonomy, you learn to suppress your preferences just to survive. And that suppression doesn't go away on its own. You have to actively reclaim it.

What to do when you don't know what you want

This is the question I get most often. "Kalesh, I want to make decisions from preference, but I don't know what my preferences are. I feel nothing. How do I choose?"

First, let me validate that. It makes sense. If you've spent years or decades ignoring your preferences, they're going to be quiet. They might not even exist as felt sensations yet. They might exist only as intellectual ideas. And that's okay.

Here's what I've found helpful. Start with the negative. What do you not want? What do you actively dislike? Sometimes it's easier to feel the "no" before you can feel the "yes." Your body might have a stronger reaction to what's wrong than to what's right. And that's a starting point.

Then work your way toward the positive. What do you tolerate? What do you prefer? What do you actually want? The gradient matters. You don't have to jump from "I don't know" to "I know exactly what I want." You can land somewhere in between.

Another thing that helps is to slow down. Way down. When you're making a decision from fear, your brain wants to rush. It wants to get the choice over with so you can return to safety. But when you're making a decision from preference, you need time. You need space to feel into the options, to notice how your body responds, to let the preference signal emerge.

I use a practice I call "the three breaths rule." Before I make any decision, even a small one, I take three slow breaths. I feel my feet on the ground. I notice what's happening in my chest, my belly, my throat. And then I ask myself: "If I had no fear, what would I choose?" The answer that comes isn't always right. But it's a starting point. It's a direction.

A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible is Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link). Nedra Glover Tawwab's work helped me see that boundaries aren't about pushing people away. They're about making space for your preferences. They're about saying "this is what I want" and "this is what I don't want" in a way that honors your own needs.

You won't always get it right

I want to be honest with you about something. Making decisions from preference doesn't mean you'll always get what you want. It doesn't mean you'll never be disappointed. It doesn't mean you'll never make a "wrong" choice. You will. I do. All the time.

But here's the difference. When you make a decision from fear and it goes wrong, your nervous system says "see, I told you. You can't handle this. You should have stayed small." It reinforces the fear. It tightens the grip.

When you make a decision from preference and it goes wrong, your nervous system says "that was uncomfortable, but I survived. I can try again." It builds resilience. It loosens the grip.

That's the real work. Not making perfect decisions. Not always knowing what you want. But building a relationship with your own preferences that is patient, curious, and kind. You're teaching your body that it's allowed to want things. That wanting is not dangerous. That preference is not selfish. That you get to choose what you want, even when the choice is hard, even when the outcome is uncertain, even when your fear parts are screaming at you to stop.

Sit with that.

Notice what just happened in your body. Did you tense up? Did you relax? Did you feel a small opening or a closing? That's your system talking. That's your preference signal, maybe faint, maybe buried, but there. It's been there all along. It just needs you to listen.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Set Boundaries, Find Peace (paid link) - A practical guide to boundaries that many find accessible
  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link) - One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate
  • Adult Children of Emotionally Immature Parents (paid link) - A book that many find validates their childhood experience
